            print(f"  ({r},{c}): {before[r][c]} -> {after[r][c]}")

# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== Key nodes ===")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in 'MOGpw':
        print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} hash={node['hash'][:8]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- State 27: white pixels at (15-18, 51-53), gray pixels at (35-37, 44-47)
Clicking (16, 49) moves from 26 to 27, clicking (36, 45) moves from 27 to 26.
But this is just toggling between two states, not making progress. I need to find a different action.
Looking at the current state (state 26), I have:

- Gray pixels at (15-18, 51-53)
- Purple square at (31-35, 42-46) with M at (33, 44)
- Orange object at (14-18, 47-51) with M at (16, 49)

The white pixels at (35-37, 44-47) are near the purple square. Maybe I need to click the purple square M center at (33, 44) to move it to cover the white pixels, completing them.
But I already tried clicking (33, 44) and it was no_impact. Let me check if the purple square is at a different position now.

Wait, let me look at the gray pixels id=26 at (29-31, 42-44). These are 6 pixels, which is the same as the gray pixels id=31 that I saw before. Maybe these are the target gray pixels that the purple square needs to cover.
Let me try clicking the purple square M center at (33, 44) again, but this time with intent='solve' to bypass the no_impact check.
Actually, no. The no_impact check is correct - clicking (33, 44) doesn't change the gameplay state. I need to find a different action.
Let me look at the orange diamond at the bottom (48-54, 37-43). It has orange pixels but no M center. Maybe I need to click one of the orange pixels to activate it.
Actually, looking at the pattern from before, I clicked the purple ring center first. The purple ring is at (15-21, 54-60). Let me check if it's still there.
Looking at the segmentation, I see purple pixels (p) at various positions, but they're all 1-pixel objects. The purple ring seems to be made of individual pixels.
